title: Reconvergence-driven cut — priority changes the final cut

steps.title: What this primitive picks at each step
steps.narrative:
Abc_NodeGetLeafCostOne (abcReconv.c:126) returns the number of brand-new leaves added by popping a leaf and replacing it with its two fanins:

cost(leaf) = (¬fanin0.in_cone_or_leaves) + (¬fanin1.in_cone_or_leaves)

Cost 0 shrinks |L| by 1, cost 1 keeps |L|, cost 2 grows |L| by 1. PIs return 999.

Abc_NodeBuildCutLevelOne_int picks the leaf with lowest cost, ties by deepest level, and stops once |L| + cost − 1 > nNodeSizeMax. That priority is the "prefer reconvergent" bias.

This walkthrough demonstrates that the priority changes the final cut — not just the order of expansion. With nNodeSizeMax = 4, cost-based and naive level-based pickers end up with different leaf sets and different cone sizes.

steps.title: Initial: L = {X, Y}, cone = {T}
steps.narrative: Seed the leaf set with T's two fanins. fMarkB is set on every node currently in the cone or on a leaf — that's the predicate the cost function reads to test "already in".

panes.body: Tie at cost 2. Tie-break by deepest level: X level 2 > Y level 1 → pick X.

steps.title: Iter 1: pop X, push u, v
steps.narrative: Pop X from the leaves; absorb it into the cone. Push X's fanins as new leaves: u and v. fMarkB on both. |L|: 2 → 3 (cost 2 = 2 pushed − 1 popped + 1 boundary).

steps.title: Iter 2: still no reconvergence — pick u by tie-break
steps.narrative: All three leaves cost 2 — none of them have fanins in L yet. Tie-break by deepest level: u, v, Y are all at level 1. Further tie broken by id; say u wins.

steps.title: Iter 2 expand: pop u, push a, c — now a is marked
steps.narrative: Pop u, push fanins a and c. |L| = 4 = cap. Critically, a is now in the leaf set — that exposes a reconvergence at Y, which has a as one of its fanins.

steps.title: Iter 3 cost re-eval — reconvergence at Y!
steps.narrative:
Y's fanin a is now marked. So Y's cost drops to 1, beating every other expandable candidate.

This is the moment the priority earns its keep. Two leaves sit at the same level (v and Y, both level 1) but with different costs. Cap-budget allows the cost-1 expansion (4 + 1 − 1 = 4 ≤ 4) but not the cost-2 expansion (4 + 2 − 1 = 5 > 4).

entries.Why: a is shared with the cone — only b is a fresh leaf

panes.title: Naive level-based — what would happen?
panes.body: Without the cost preference, tie-break by level alone among non-PI leaves picks between v (level 1) and Y (level 1) — say v by id. cost(v) = 2 → cap-budget exceeds → STOP. Final leaves would be {a, c, v, Y}, cone = {T, X, u}. Y stays as a leaf rather than being absorbed.

steps.title: Iter 3 expand: pop Y, push only b (a already marked)
steps.narrative:
Pop Y. Of Y's fanins, a is already in fMarkB — the push-only-if-not-marked check at abcReconv.c:357..362 skips it. Only b gets added.

|L| stays at 4. Y has been absorbed into the cone — the priority's payoff.

steps.title: Iter 4: only v is expandable, cost 2 — STOP
steps.narrative: PIs (a, b, c) cost 999. Only v can expand, but cost 2 + |L| − 1 = 5 > cap = 4. STOP.

steps.title: Final cut — and how it compares to the no-priority path
steps.narrative:
The cost-based walk lands at leaves {a, b, c, v}, cone {T, X, u, Y} (4 internal nodes). Compare to the level-based walk, which would have stopped one iteration earlier because v (cost 2) exceeded the cap before Y could be reached.

Refactor and resub now operate on the cost-based cut. The priority bought one extra cone node (Y) and a smaller-on-the-circuit leaf footprint at the same |L|.

panes.title: Priority vs no-priority — final cuts diverge
Why this matters
panes.body: Refactor sees different cones in the two paths — different sets of cone nodes mean different Boolean functions to refactor and different MFFCs to compute. Cost-based gets to refactor a 4-node cone implementing (u·v) · (a·b); level-based only gets the 3-node u·v cone with Y still external. On bigger circuits the priority unlocks meaningfully larger reconv cones for downstream synthesis.
